refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 as stated is a KPI support requirement but lacks specificity on measurement scope, boundary conditions, and operational context. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a measurable outcome dependent on operator behavior, vehicle efficiency, and system-level optimization. The requirement now explicitly bounds the evaluation period and scenario scope, making it verifiable and traceable to design decisions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish baseline energy consumption and ton-moved metrics during acceptance testing across representative worksite scenarios (e.g., load types, terrain, operator skill levels). Compare measured energy_per_ton against E_target. Require telemetry capture and post-analysis to isolate vehicle efficiency, operational losses, and system-level optimization contributions.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit load moved through efficient drivetrain, hydraulic, and powertrain design.

FR-EE-002: The teleoperation system shall provide operator feedback and control responsiveness to enable efficient task execution without energy waste due to control latency or poor situational awareness.
FR-EE-003: The NGHE solution shall collect, aggregate, and report energy consumption and productivity metrics (energy_per_ton) in real-time and post-mission for performance tracking and optimization.
FR-EE-004: Safety services shall not impose energy penalties that degrade energy_per_ton below E_target when safety functions are active.

candidate_dps: DP-EE-A: Optimize vehicle powertrain efficiency (engine tuning, transmission efficiency, hydraulic circuit design) and implement real-time energy monitoring and reporting via telemetry to enable operator awareness and system-level optimization feedback loops.

DP-EE-B: Implement a closed-loop energy optimization system that monitors energy_per_ton in real-time, provides operator coaching via teleoperation interface, and adjusts vehicle operating parameters (e.g., engine RPM, load distribution) to maintain E_target.

DP-EE-C: Decompose energy efficiency into vehicle hardware efficiency (DP), operator behavior optimization (teleoperation DP), and system-level energy accounting and reporting (NGHE solution DP), with explicit allocation of energy budget to each subsystem.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-EE-C: Decompose energy efficiency into vehicle hardware efficiency (DP), operator behavior optimization (teleoperation DP), and system-level energy accounting and reporting (NGHE solution DP), with explicit allocation of energy budget to each subsystem.

potential_sources: Vehicle powertrain efficiency and teleoperation control responsiveness are coupled: poor teleoperation latency or feedback can cause operator over-correction and energy waste, masking vehicle efficiency gains.

Safety services and energy efficiency are coupled: emergency braking, load-limiting, and stability control may consume energy; design must ensure safety does not degrade energy_per_ton below acceptable threshold.

Operator skill and energy efficiency are coupled: teleoperation coaching and situational awareness directly influence energy consumption; system must provide real-time feedback to guide efficient operation.
coupling_check.notes: DP-EE-C acknowledges these couplings by explicitly allocating energy efficiency responsibility across vehicle, teleoperation, and system layers. Coupling is managed through clear interface contracts (telemetry, tasking, safety alerts) and energy budget allocation. Residual coupling risk is acceptable if managed via integration testing and operator training.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: E_target is achievable with current vehicle platform and operator skill levels. Risk: If E_target is overly aggressive, design may require vehicle redesign or operator training beyond project scope.

Assumption: Real-time energy monitoring and reporting are feasible via existing telemetry infrastructure. Risk: If telemetry bandwidth or latency is insufficient, energy accounting may be delayed or incomplete, reducing feedback effectiveness.

Assumption: Operator behavior can be influenced via teleoperation coaching without degrading safety or task completion time. Risk: If coaching is intrusive or distracting, operator may disable feedback, negating energy optimization benefit.

Assumption: Safety services can be designed to minimize energy penalty. Risk: If safety functions (e.g., load-limiting, stability control) are inherently energy-intensive, energy_per_ton may not meet E_target even with optimized vehicle and operator behavior.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-EE-C to explicitly define energy budget allocation across vehicle (e.g., 70% efficiency target), teleoperation (e.g., <5% control latency penalty), and system-level optimization (e.g., <10% reporting/coaching overhead). Establish energy margin (e.g., 10% buffer) to account for variability in operator skill and worksite conditions. Require trade study to validate feasibility of E_target against allocated budgets.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level energy efficiency orchestration, including real-time energy accounting, telemetry aggregation, operator coaching via teleoperation interface, and energy_per_ton reporting. Owns the integration of vehicle efficiency, teleoperation responsiveness, and safety service energy impacts into a unified energy management system.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional telemetry stream carrying vehicle energy consumption (kWh, fuel consumption), productivity metrics (ton_moved, cycle time), and operator performance data (control inputs, response time). NGHE Solution receives raw telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation modules, aggregates into energy_per_ton KPI, and sends coaching feedback back to Teleoperation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Task definition and priority signals from Site Manager and Tele-operator specifying load type, destination, and energy efficiency priority level. NGHE Solution uses tasking context to adjust energy optimization strategy and set operator coaching intensity.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety event notifications from Safety Services module (e.g., load-limiting engaged, stability control active). NGHE Solution receives alerts to account for energy penalties incurred by safety functions and may adjust energy_per_ton target or operator coaching in response.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for all telemetry, tasking, and safety alert exchanges. NGHE Solution depends on Network Link for real-time energy data delivery and operator feedback transmission.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of system-level energy efficiency orchestration, energy accounting, and KPI reporting. It integrates data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert). Minimal diffusion: energy accounting logic is centralized; operator coaching is delivered via Teleoperation interface. High containment score reflects clear ownership of energy management responsibility.

Vehicle owns powertrain efficiency and energy consumption measurement. However, energy_per_ton KPI calculation and optimization strategy are system-level concerns that belong in NGHE Solution. If energy accounting logic is distributed across Vehicle and NGHE Solution, diffusion risk increases (e.g., inconsistent energy metrics, duplicated calculations). Vehicle should expose energy telemetry via Telemetry interface; NGHE Solution aggregates and optimizes.

Teleoperation owns operator control interface and feedback delivery. Operator coaching (e.g., 'reduce engine RPM for efficiency') is delivered via Teleoperation. However, coaching logic and energy optimization strategy should reside in NGHE Solution, not Teleoperation. If coaching logic is embedded in Teleoperation, it becomes difficult to adjust energy targets or integrate safety constraints. Teleoperation should receive coaching commands from NGHE Solution via Telemetry interface.

Safety Services owns safety-critical functions (load-limiting, stability control) but does not own energy efficiency optimization. If energy accounting or optimization logic is placed in Safety Services, it creates tight coupling and diffusion. Safety Services should report energy-impacting events via SafetyAlert interface; NGHE Solution accounts for energy penalties and adjusts targets accordingly.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner of energy efficiency KPI management because it: (1) has system-level visibility into vehicle efficiency, operator behavior, and safety impacts; (2) can aggregate energy telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services without duplication; (3) can implement energy optimization strategy and operator coaching logic in a centralized, maintainable manner; (4) can adjust energy targets and coaching in response to changing worksite conditions and safety constraints. Containment is high (0.95) because energy accounting and optimization are inherently system-level concerns, not vehicle-specific or operator-specific.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution energy accounting logic is not synchronized with Vehicle telemetry generation, energy_per_ton calculations may be inconsistent or delayed. Mitigation: Define clear telemetry schema and timing contracts via Telemetry interface specification.

Risk: If operator coaching is too aggressive or frequent, operator may disable feedback or ignore recommendations, negating energy optimization benefit. Mitigation: Implement adaptive coaching intensity based on operator response and task context; require human factors validation.

Risk: If Safety Services energy penalties are not accurately reported via SafetyAlert, NGHE Solution may set unrealistic energy targets that conflict with safety requirements. Mitigation: Require Safety Services to quantify energy impact of each safety function; NGHE Solution must account for these impacts in energy budget allocation.

Risk: If Network Link latency or bandwidth is insufficient, real-time energy accounting and operator coaching may be delayed, reducing effectiveness. Mitigation: Define latency and bandwidth requirements for Telemetry and Tasking interfaces; validate during integration testing.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit energy accounting interface contract between NGHE Solution and Vehicle module, specifying telemetry schema, measurement units, reporting frequency, and accuracy requirements. Define energy budget allocation across vehicle efficiency, teleoperation control latency, and system-level optimization overhead. Require Safety Services to provide energy impact quantification for each safety function (e.g., 'load-limiting reduces energy_per_ton by 5%'). Implement adaptive operator coaching logic in NGHE Solution with feedback mechanisms to measure coaching effectiveness and adjust intensity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Refined FR-EE (energy efficiency KPI) is clearly mapped to DP-EE-C (decomposed energy efficiency across vehicle, teleoperation, and system layers). DP-EE-C directly addresses the FR by allocating energy efficiency responsibility to specific subsystems and establishing energy accounting and optimization mechanisms. The mapping is traceable: FR specifies energy_per_ton ≤ E_target; DP specifies how to achieve this through vehicle efficiency, operator behavior optimization, and system-level energy management. No logical gaps or contradictions identified.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Energy accounting logic could be diffused across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution if interface contracts are not clearly defined. Mitigation: Establish explicit telemetry schema and energy metric definitions in Telemetry interface specification.

Risk: Operator coaching logic could be diffused across Teleoperation and NGHE Solution if responsibility boundaries are unclear. Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns coaching strategy and sends coaching commands to Teleoperation via Telemetry interface; Teleoperation owns only UI/UX delivery.

Risk: Energy optimization strategy could be diffused across NGHE Solution and Safety Services if safety-energy trade-offs are not managed. Mitigation: Safety Services reports energy impacts via SafetyAlert; NGHE Solution owns energy budget allocation and optimization strategy.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for energy efficiency KPI management. Containment score of 0.95 reflects centralized ownership of energy accounting, optimization strategy, and operator coaching logic. Diffusion score of 0.05 indicates minimal risk of logic duplication or scattered responsibility. Key to success is clear interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) that define data exchange and responsibility boundaries between NGHE Solution and Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-EE is feasible via acceptance testing that measures energy_per_ton across representative worksite scenarios. Telemetry interface provides real-time energy consumption and productivity data. NGHE Solution energy accounting module can aggregate and report energy_per_ton KPI. Verification requires baseline establishment, controlled testing, and post-mission analysis. Feasibility is high if telemetry infrastructure is reliable and energy metrics are accurately defined.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of energy efficiency responsibility is clear: Vehicle owns powertrain efficiency (DP-EE-A); Teleoperation owns operator control responsiveness and feedback delivery (DP-EE-B); NGHE Solution owns energy accounting, optimization strategy, and KPI reporting (DP-EE-C). Each subsystem has explicit energy efficiency targets or constraints. Allocation is traceable from FR-EE to sub-FRs to DPs to IMs. No ambiguity in ownership or responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Establish energy budget allocation across vehicle (e.g., 70% efficiency target), teleoperation (e.g., <5% control latency penalty), and system-level optimization (e.g., <10% reporting/coaching overhead). Require trade study to validate feasibility of E_target against allocated budgets.

Define explicit telemetry schema and energy metric definitions in Telemetry interface specification, including measurement units, reporting frequency, accuracy requirements, and timestamp synchronization.

Require Safety Services to quantify energy impact of each safety function (e.g., 'load-limiting reduces energy_per_ton by 5%') and report via SafetyAlert interface. NGHE Solution must account for these impacts in energy budget allocation.
Implement adaptive operator coaching logic in NGHE Solution with feedback mechanisms to measure coaching effectiveness and adjust intensity based on operator response and task context.

Establish acceptance criteria for energy_per_ton KPI, including measurement methodology, evaluation period, scenario coverage, and acceptable variance. Require baseline establishment and controlled testing to validate E_target achievement.

decision.justification: Refined FR-EE and selected DP-EE-C are well-formed and traceable to NGHE Solution IM boundary. However, the requirement is not atomic and has been decomposed into four sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-004) that require further refinement and design. Each sub-FR addresses a specific aspect of energy efficiency (vehicle efficiency, teleoperation responsiveness, energy accounting, safety-energy trade-offs) and must be decomposed to the next level to establish detailed design parameters and implementation modules. Proceeding to next level decomposition is necessary to establish concrete design decisions, interface specifications, and verification criteria.
decision.justification_detail: The current level establishes the system-level energy efficiency strategy and allocates responsibility across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules. However, detailed design decisions remain open: (1) What specific powertrain efficiency improvements will Vehicle implement? (2) What control latency and feedback mechanisms will Teleoperation provide? (3) What energy accounting algorithms and coaching logic will NGHE Solution implement? (4) How will Safety Services minimize energy penalties? These questions require next-level decomposition to answer.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit load moved through efficient drivetrain, hydraulic, and powertrain design.

FR-EE-002: The teleoperation system shall provide operator feedback and control responsiveness to enable efficient task execution without energy waste due to control latency or poor situational awareness.
FR-EE-003: The NGHE solution shall collect, aggregate, and report energy consumption and productivity metrics (energy_per_ton) in real-time and post-mission for performance tracking and optimization.
FR-EE-004: Safety services shall not impose energy penalties that degrade energy_per_ton below E_target when safety functions are active.

assumptions: E_target is achievable with current vehicle platform and operator skill levels.
Real-time energy monitoring and reporting are feasible via existing telemetry infrastructure.
Operator behavior can be influenced via teleoperation coaching without degrading safety or task completion time.
Safety services can be designed to minimize energy penalty.
Energy efficiency is a measurable outcome dependent on operator behavior, vehicle efficiency, and system-level optimization.
Telemetry interface can reliably deliver energy consumption and productivity data with sufficient frequency and accuracy.
NGHE Solution has system-level visibility into vehicle efficiency, operator behavior, and safety impacts.
open_risks: E_target may be overly aggressive, requiring vehicle redesign or operator training beyond project scope.
Telemetry bandwidth or latency may be insufficient for real-time energy accounting and operator coaching.
Operator coaching may be intrusive or distracting, causing operator to disable feedback and negate energy optimization benefit.
Safety functions may be inherently energy-intensive, preventing energy_per_ton from meeting E_target.
Energy accounting logic may be diffused across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution if interface contracts are not clearly defined.
Operator coaching logic may be diffused across Teleoperation and NGHE Solution if responsibility boundaries are unclear.
Energy optimization strategy may be diffused across NGHE Solution and Safety Services if safety-energy trade-offs are not managed.
Network Link latency or bandwidth may be insufficient for real-time energy accounting and operator coaching.
evidence_needed: Trade study validating feasibility of E_target against allocated energy budgets (vehicle efficiency, teleoperation latency, system-level optimization overhead).
Telemetry interface specification defining energy metric schema, measurement units, reporting frequency, and accuracy requirements.
Safety Services energy impact quantification for each safety function (e.g., 'load-limiting reduces energy_per_ton by 5%').
Operator coaching effectiveness study measuring impact of coaching on energy_per_ton and operator acceptance.
Network Link latency and bandwidth analysis validating feasibility of real-time energy accounting and operator feedback.
Acceptance test plan and criteria for energy_per_ton KPI, including measurement methodology, evaluation period, scenario coverage, and acceptable variance.
Baseline energy consumption and productivity data from representative worksite scenarios to establish E_target feasibility.
